learn.title: Solar as Architecture
learn.intro: BIPV turns buildings into power plants, blending aesthetics with clean energy. It reduces material costs and carbon footprints while maximizing urban solar potential.
learn.c1_text: BIPV integrates solar cells into windows, roofs, and walls. For example, a solar glass window can generate 100-200W per square meter, powering LED lights or cooling systems.
learn.c2_text: Unlike traditional panels, BIPV modules are part of the building's structure. A 2023 study showed BIPV façades can reduce energy bills by 30% while cutting embodied carbon by 25%.
learn.c3_text: India’s cities could install 10 GW of BIPV by 2030, per MNRE targets. Mumbai’s upcoming solar skyscrapers aim to cut grid demand by 15%.
learn.stat2: BIPV costs 10-20% more upfront but saves 15-30% over 20 years.
learn.stat4: India’s BIPV potential: 150 GW rooftop + 50 GW façades.

learn.title: H2 from Water
learn.intro: PEM and alkaline electrolyzers split water into hydrogen. PEM is efficient for renewables; alkaline suits large-scale projects.
learn.c1_text: PEM electrolyzers use proton membranes and operate at 70-90% efficiency. They start/stop rapidly, ideal for wind/solar farms. A 1 MW PEM unit produces 12 kg H2/hour.
learn.c2_text: Alkaline systems use liquid electrolyte, costing 20-30% less than PEM. They require steady power (e.g., 24/7 grid supply) and produce 20-30 kg H2/hour per MW.
learn.c3_text: India targets 5 GW renewable-linked H2 by 2030. PEM dominates pilot projects (e.g., NTPC’s 200 MW plant), while alkaline suits industrial hubs.

learn.title: Power from Both Sides
learn.intro: Bifacial panels capture sunlight on both sides, boosting output by 10-30%. They thrive in reflective environments like deserts or snow.
learn.c1_text: Bifacial panels generate power from front and back. A 350W bifacial panel can produce 15-20% more energy than a monofacial panel in optimal conditions.
learn.c2_text: Albedo (reflectivity) matters. A white roof boosts backside yield by 30%, while gravel adds 15%. A 2022 study showed bifacial gains average 12-18% globally.
learn.c3_text: India’s 90 GW solar target includes 25% bifacial panels by 2030. Rajasthan’s Bhadla Solar Park uses bifacial tech to hit 2.2 GW with 12% higher efficiency.
learn.stat2: Bifacial panels cost 5-10% more but yield 15-25% more energy.

learn.title: Battery Second Life
learn.intro: Used EV batteries store grid energy, extending their lifespan and cutting waste. They’re cheaper than new batteries for stationary storage.
learn.c1_text: After 8-10 years in EVs, batteries retain 70-80% capacity. They’re repurposed for grid storage, reducing costs by 40% vs. new lithium-ion systems.
learn.c2_text: Batteries must meet 70% capacity, 90% cycle life, and 10-year warranty. AI monitors health, ensuring safe integration. A 2023 report found 95% of EV batteries qualify.
learn.c3_text: India’s EV battery recycling policy (2022) mandates 50% reuse before recycling. NTPC plans 500 MWh grid storage using retired EV batteries by 2025.
learn.stat2: EV battery reuse cuts grid storage costs by 40-50%.
learn.stat3: Global EV battery stock: 1.2 million tons (2023)
learn.stat4: India’s EV battery recycling target: 50% reuse by 2030

learn.title: India’s Green Hydrogen Plan
learn.intro: India’s National Green Hydrogen Mission aims to make the country a global H2 hub. It offers subsidies, R&D support, and targets 5 GW by 2030.
learn.c1_text: India plans 5 GW renewable-powered green hydrogen by 2030. The mission includes 4 GW for export and 1 GW for domestic use (e.g., steel, fertilizers).
learn.c2_text: Producers get ₹150/kg H2 subsidy (vs. ₹300 market price). Capital expenditure (CAPEX) support covers 20% of project costs. A 2023 pilot in Gujarat produced H2 at ₹120/kg.
learn.c3_text: India’s mission aligns with MNRE’s 2023 roadmap. It aims to reduce import dependence (90% of H2 is imported) and create 100,000 jobs in green H2 sectors.

learn.title: Wave Energy Tech
learn.intro: Wave energy converters (WECs) harness ocean motion. Technologies include point absorbers, oscillating water columns, and buoys. They’re promising but face durability challenges.
learn.c1_text: Point absorbers use buoys to capture wave energy. A 1 MW device can power 1,000 homes. The UK’s O2 Energy project generated 2 MW in 2022.
learn.c2_text: OWC systems use air pressure from waves to drive turbines. A 2021 study found they achieve 25-30% efficiency. The European Marine Energy Centre tested a 1.2 MW OWC in Portugal.
learn.c3_text: India’s 7,000 km coastline has 126 GW wave energy potential. The National Institute of Ocean Technology (NIOT) is testing WECs in the Andaman Sea, targeting 50 MW by 2030.
